_comment: Declared intent for the CI Quality Gate: the exact set of ci.yml jobs whose
result must be aggregated by the `ci-gate` job. This is the SPEC half of a
spec-vs-reality cross-check — scripts/verify-ci-gate.sh asserts that the
`ci-gate` job's `needs:` array in .github/workflows/ci.yml matches this set
exactly (and that every job named here actually exists in ci.yml).
The duplication with the workflow's `needs:` list is DELIBERATE: it is the
redundancy that lets the drift check catch a stray edit to `needs:` that would
otherwise silently drop a security-critical job out of the gate. Do not derive
this file from ci.yml — that would make the check circular and meaningless.
`changes` is included: it is the path-filter engine the gate explicitly asserts
succeeded (`changes.result == success`), so it is a load-bearing gate, not
background infrastructure. `ci-gate` itself is NOT listed (a gate cannot gate
For the SIX jobs deliberately EXCLUDED from this gate and the rationale, see
docs/project/ci-gating.md — 'What the gate does not cover'. In short: PR-only
procedural checks (semver-check, check-commit-messages) and tag-scoped checks
(validate-changelog) do not run on push events and would deadlock the staging

jobs.why: Path-filter engine; the gate asserts this job == success before trusting any skip.
Static analysis / lint gate (warnings as errors).
Build & test on Linux/macOS/Windows; core correctness.
Secret leak scan over full git history.
XSD contract fragments fresh + correctly wired (design_docs).
Crate-source D-NNNN decision references resolve to the register.
GitHub Actions workflow validation (also runs this drift check).
Declared MSRV compiles and passes checks.
Packaging health; gates staging/main pushes, skipped on PRs.
